skill.name: mobile-games
skill.description: Guidance for designing mobile games around touch controls, battery use, thermal limits, performance targets, and app store requirements.
skill.summary: Plan mobile games that respect touch input, battery life, device heat, screens, and store rules.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static weak cryptography and reconnaissance findings are false positives caused by ordinary mobile game documentation text. No prompt injection, malware intent, credential access, command execution, or network exfiltration was found. The skill does declare filesystem tools, so publication should disclose that it can guide file reads and edits.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged text is the skill description about mobile game development. It does not mention or use a weak cryptographic algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain metadata describing mobile game topics. There is no code, cryptographic API, hash function, cipher, or instruction to implement cryptography.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged table heading compares touch input with desktop and console input. It does not describe cryptographic behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 31 is a Markdown table header for control schemes. The context is user input design, not encryption or hashing.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged phrase explains that touch input can cover the screen. It has no cryptographic meaning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 34 is documentation about touch-screen occlusion. There is no executable code or weak algorithm reference.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The first duplicate line 101 finding refers to an anti-pattern about desktop controls on mobile. It is unrelated to cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 101 is a design recommendation in a Markdown table. The text contains no cipher, hash, key handling, or security instruction.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The second duplicate line 101 finding points to the same benign design anti-pattern text. No separate security issue is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both static hits on line 101 resolve to the same mobile UI guidance. I found no evidence of cryptographic use or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged line advises avoiding precise timing requirements in mobile controls. It does not collect system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 42 is gameplay design advice. There are no commands, APIs, or instructions for enumerating files, devices, users, or processes.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged line is a section heading for Android store requirements. It does not instruct host or device reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 76 only names Android and Google Play context. It contains no probing behavior, system inventory logic, or data collection instruction.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Access Declared
low_findings.description: The skill declares Read, Write, Edit, Glob, and Grep tools. This is legitimate for game development assistance but should be disclosed to users.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools metadata explicitly includes filesystem read and edit capabilities. The surrounding skill content is benign, so the concern is disclosure rather than malicious behavior.

content.user_title: Design Mobile Games for Touch and Performance
content.value_statement: Mobile games often fail when they reuse desktop controls or ignore device limits. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan touch-first games with better performance and store readiness.
actual_capabilities: Explains mobile platform constraints for touch, battery, heat, screen size, and interruptions.
Recommends touch input practices such as larger targets, feedback, gestures, and orientation support.
Guides performance choices for frame rate, sleep states, effects, network use, and battery impact.
Summarizes iOS and Android store concerns, including privacy labels, screenshots, target APIs, and app bundles.
Compares monetization models for premium, free with purchases, ads, and subscriptions.
Identifies mobile game anti-patterns, including desktop controls and always-on network behavior.
limitations: It provides planning guidance, not a complete game engine or production framework.
It does not include platform-specific store policy updates beyond the source content.
It does not implement analytics, payments, ads, or multiplayer services.
It requires user review for exact iOS and Google Play submission requirements.

use_cases.title: Convert a Desktop Game Concept
use_cases.description: Adapt a mouse or controller game idea into a touch-first mobile design with clear control changes.

use_cases.title: Audit Mobile Performance Plans
use_cases.description: Review a mobile game plan for battery drain, thermal risk, frame rate targets, and pause behavior.

use_cases.title: Prepare Store Submission Work
use_cases.description: Create a checklist for privacy labels, screenshots, account deletion, Android API targets, and app bundles.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Mobile Game Idea
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this mobile game idea for touch controls, screen size, interruptions, battery use, and orientation support. List the main changes needed.

prompt_templates.title: Design Touch Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a touch control scheme for this game. Include hit target sizes, feedback, gestures, occlusion risks, and portrait or landscape tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize for Device Limits
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this mobile game feature list for performance, battery, thermal, network, and pause behavior. Recommend concrete reductions for low-end devices.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Store-Ready Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a mobile release readiness plan for iOS and Android. Cover screenshots, privacy labels, account deletion, target APIs, app bundles, and monetization risks.

output_examples.input: A platformer uses small virtual buttons and demands frame-perfect jumps.
output_examples.output: The review would recommend larger touch targets, forgiving timing windows, haptic or visual feedback, and optional gesture controls.

output_examples.input: A puzzle game keeps networking active while paused.
output_examples.output: The audit would flag battery impact and suggest caching, background sleep behavior, and sync only when needed.

output_examples.input: A team is preparing an iOS and Android launch checklist.
output_examples.output: The plan would cover privacy labels, account deletion rules, screenshot coverage, Android target API, 64-bit support, and app bundle packaging.

best_practices: Start with touch-first controls before adapting desktop or console mechanics.
Define performance budgets for frame rate, battery, heat, network use, and paused states.
Check current App Store and Google Play rules before release.
anti_patterns: Do not copy desktop controls directly onto a phone screen.
Do not ignore battery drain, heat, or background interruptions.
Do not require an always-on network connection without a clear gameplay need.

faq.question: Does this skill build a full mobile game?
faq.answer: No. It provides design and planning guidance for mobile game constraints and release readiness.

faq.question: Can it help with touch control design?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers target size, feedback, gestures, screen occlusion, and orientation choices.

faq.question: Does it include performance guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes frame rate, sleep states, thermal reduction, effects, and network usage guidance.

faq.question: Does it cover both iOS and Android?
faq.answer: Yes. It summarizes important iOS App Store and Android Google Play considerations.

faq.question: Does it provide current store policy guarantees?
faq.answer: No. Store policies change, so teams should verify current rules before submission.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention filesystem access?
faq.answer: The skill declares read and edit tools. Users should review file changes before applying them.
